claim: Adding a file system to an agent changes behavior fundamentally—agents start following through on long tasks, staying on track, and building on prior work rather than hallucinating scattered answers
actionable: Give your agent access to a persistent file system (like Vercel's named sandboxes) and have it use a memory.md file to read and write persistent state across sessions
source_title: Give Your Agent a Computer — Nico Albanese, Vercel

claim: Named persistent sandboxes (not ephemeral) solve the lifecycle management problem—Vercel snapshots the file system after inactivity, allowing agents to pick up work from prior sessions transparently
actionable: Use Vercel's named persistent sandboxes instead of ephemeral environments; reference sandboxes by name and let the provider handle session routing and file system snapshots
source_title: Give Your Agent a Computer — Nico Albanese, Vercel

claim: Memory should be implemented as actual files in the agent's sandbox (e.g., memories.md), not in context—agents are better at reading/writing files than managing in-context memory
actionable: Create a memories.md file in the agent's persistent sandbox, fetch it in prepare_call, inject it into instructions, and let the agent append new facts via bash commands
source_title: Give Your Agent a Computer — Nico Albanese, Vercel

claim: Agents are effective at generating and executing code (Python scripts) as a feedback loop—build, run, evaluate, iterate—because there are compilers and type checkers providing deterministic feedback
actionable: Instruct agents to create reusable Python scripts for repeated tasks and store them in the sandbox with descriptions in memory.md, allowing reuse on future requests
source_title: Give Your Agent a Computer — Nico Albanese, Vercel

claim: Do NOT compact context by stripping older tool calls or messages from history—this invalidates prompt caching and wastes tokens. With million-token windows, full context is more efficient than lossy compression
actionable: Avoid manual context pruning strategies; instead use sub-agents to isolate work streams and return summaries, preserving cache continuity in the main thread
source_title: Give Your Agent a Computer — Nico Albanese, Vercel

claim: Use prepare_call() (runs once before agent starts) to inject contextual data like user ID or sandbox references into the agent's runtime context for all subsequent steps
actionable: In AI SDK v6, define call_option_schema to accept runtime parameters (like sandbox), then use prepare_call to inject them into the agent's context object for all tool calls
source_title: Give Your Agent a Computer — Nico Albanese, Vercel

claim: Provider-executed tools (like OpenAI's web search) are fast to implement but couple you to a single provider; trade freedom for convenience
actionable: Use provider-executed tools for rapid prototyping, but plan to migrate to custom or MCP tools if you need multi-provider support or offline operation
source_title: Give Your Agent a Computer — Nico Albanese, Vercel

claim: With AI SDK v6, define tool descriptions carefully—they control when agents use tools and how they use them; descriptions are the primary lever for behavior
actionable: Invest time in clear, specific tool descriptions; use concrete examples of when to use each tool if the agent is misusing them
source_title: Give Your Agent a Computer — Nico Albanese, Vercel

claim: Agents using bash as the only tool can handle 80% of tasks—they're effective at writing and executing shell commands; avoid tool sprawl
actionable: Start agents with just bash and a file system; add specialized tools only when bash proves insufficient for a category of tasks
source_title: Give Your Agent a Computer — Nico Albanese, Vercel

claim: End-to-end type safety (from agent definition through to UI components) prevents silent failures and mismatches—use TypeScript and infer types from agent tools
actionable: In AI SDK v6, use InferAgentUIMessage to derive message types from your agent definition, then apply those types to useChat hook and route handlers
source_title: Give Your Agent a Computer — Nico Albanese, Vercel
